title: Field Theory Models for a Holographic Superconductor in Two Dimensions
authors: Salvatore Santoro, Roberto Auzzi, Stefano Bolognesi
abstract: We investigate field theory models of holographic superconductors in which the condensation of the order parameter is induced by a Robin boundary condition. Assuming large-$c$ factorization, we study the phase diagram of a two-dimensional CFT deformed by a relevant double-trace perturbation. Using modular invariance, we relate the high- and low-temperature phases, reproducing analytically the results for the zero-winding sector of the holographic model. Moreover, we match the near-critical behaviour of the condensate with an effective Ginzburg--Landau field theory description. Another important feature of the holographic superconductor is the presence of vortices that carry fractional magnetic flux. We investigate a field theory toy model with similar properties and interpret it as a fractional Little--Parks effect.
summary: This paper develops a field-theoretic description of a 2D holographic superconductor, showing that the complex phase diagram of the bulk gravity model can be recovered from a deformed 2D CFT. It specifically explains the emergence of fractional magnetic flux and the Little-Parks effect through a 1+1D gauge theory toy model. The work demonstrates the power of using boundary-side modularity and effective actions to understand holographic phase transitions.
main_problem: The paper aims to reconstruct the phase diagram and condensate behavior of a 2D holographic superconductor using only the boundary field theory description.
main_result: The authors successfully map the four-phase holographic structure (including Hawking-Page and superconducting transitions) using a deformed 2D CFT and provide a field-theoretic mechanism for fractional magnetic flux and the Little-Parks effect.
method: The study employs large-c factorization, modular invariance, Hubbard-Stratonovich transformations, and an effective Ginzburg-Landau expansion to relate the bulk gravity solutions to the boundary CFT.
model_or_system: The system consists of a 2D Conformal Field Theory (CFT) on a torus, subject to a relevant double-trace deformation, which is dual to Einstein gravity in AdS3 coupled to a U(1) gauge field and a charged scalar field.
key_observables: Scalar condensate, magnetic flux (integer and fractional), temperature, entropy, and zero-momentum susceptibility.
important_parameters: Central charge (c), scaling dimension (Delta), double-trace coupling (kappa or f), temperature (T), and winding number (n).
main_assumptions: The large-c limit is assumed to suppress fluctuations and allow a mean-field description; the scalar mass is restricted to the range -1 < m^2 < -3/4.
figures_summary: Figures show the relationship between coupling and condensate, the reconstructed phase diagram in the T-kappa plane, and the suppression of the condensate as the winding number increases.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the holographic and dual field theory models, proceeds to reconstruct the phase diagram using modular invariance and effective action approaches, matches the near-critical behavior with Ginzburg-Landau theory, and concludes with a toy model explaining fractional flux and the Little-Parks effect.
why_interesting: It provides a bridge between holographic gravity and effective field theories, demonstrating how universal features of phase transitions and topological effects like fractional flux can be captured by boundary-side statistical mechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: In these plots [11] we show κ as a function of αH for the vacuum and n = 1, 2, 3 vortices. Here we set m2 = −0.9 and G = 0.1. In the left panel we set e = 0, while in the right panel we consider e = 10.
Figure 2: Here we plot the normalized flux e ΦB/(2π) = a0/n as a function of αH for the n = 1, 2 vortex solutions [11]. Here we set e = 10, m2 = −0.9 and GN = 0.1.
Figure 3: Phase diagram for n = 0 with m2 = −0.9 (corresponding to ∆= 0.68) and GN = 0.1 [11]. There are four lines departing from a quadruple point dividing the diagram into four distinct phases.
Figure 4: Schematic phase diagram in the (−f, T) plane under the assumptions discussed in this subsection, with the scaling dimension fixed to ∆= 1 − √

Figure 5: Comparison between the holographic condensate αH and the field theory prediction ⟨O⟩ near criticality. Top: BTZ-with-hair branch at different fixed temperatures, fitted with the high- temperature expression (3.77) as a function of the distance from κc(T). Bottom: low-temperature solitonic branch, fitted with (3.74) as a function of the distance from κv. The lower panels show the corresponding residuals.
